abstract: The long-recognized potential for the cultural and creative industries (CCIs) to drive national growth has transitioned from the UK’s landmark 1998 mapping report to contemporary "sector deals" integrated into overarching industrial strategies. This chapter investigates how these dominant CCI policy narratives manifest within local governance by examining two city-regional pilot programs for Local Industrial Strategies (LIS) in the West Midlands and the North West of England. Utilizing discourse analysis, the study scrutinizes the social practices and rhetorical flourishes that define "the local," uncovering the complex boundary work—the conceptual distinctions used by policy actors to categorize space and agency—inherent in the planning process. The findings highlight a shift toward contractualism between central and local authorities. Following Paasi’s geographic frameworks, the chapter identifies specific discursive interactions that delineate the borders of these city-regions, ultimately demonstrating the significant power of "place" to exert agency even within centrally mandated policy instruments designed for sub-national economic development.
